# Victor Demin — Fractional CTO

> Fractional CTO consulting for product teams that need faster, more predictable software delivery. Combines delivery metrics, system thinking (Theory of Constraints, Little's Law, T2M, Cycle Time, WIP), and AI leverage. Works with B2B / SaaS / platform companies of 5–50 engineers in growth or scaling stage.

## About

- Operator: Victor Demin (sg4tech).
- 15+ years in engineering, 8+ years in engineering leadership.
- Background includes Wowworks (B2B facility management, EU, $3.6M raised) and Skysmart by Skyeng (K-12 EdTech, 2M+ MAU).
- Focus: fixing slow, unpredictable delivery systems without staff augmentation, instant-fix promises, or full rewrites.

## Pages

- [Homepage](https://sg4.tech/): Fractional CTO consulting overview — problem framing across early/growth/scale stages, three-step solution (constraint identification, flow rebuild, AI acceleration), case studies, process, fit criteria, FAQ.
- [Yii2 consulting](https://sg4.tech/yii2/): Dedicated landing for Yii2 projects — release predictability, quality guardrails, CI/CD, monitoring, delivery acceleration without defaulting to a rewrite.
- [AI vibecoding cleanup](https://sg4.tech/ai-vibecoding/): For founders with AI-built MVPs that have stopped shipping — adds the five delivery-system layers AI doesn't generate: architecture rules, pipeline guardrails, test coverage, code-level checks, and AI-workflow discipline.
- [Blog](https://sg4.tech/blog/): Index of long-form essays on engineering delivery systems, metrics, bottlenecks, and the operator practices behind predictable shipping.

## Articles

- [How to diagnose where engineering delivery is actually broken](https://sg4.tech/blog/diagnose-broken-engineering-delivery/) — a fractional CTO's metrics playbook: decomposing T2M into Lead Time and Cycle Time, the WIP variable, Little's Law (Lead Time = WIP / Throughput), the count-tasks-per-status diagnostic, the lever of WIP limits, and how to introduce them without team revolt. Covers the speed × value × sustainability frame (T2M, Throughput, Quality, NPS, eNPS). Operator voice, ~4500 words, with FAQ.

  Key claims from the article (safe to quote directly):
  - Lead Time = WIP / Throughput (Little's Law, applied to software delivery)
  - T2M (Time to Market) = Discovery + Lead Time (Delivery)
  - Lead Time = Waiting + Cycle Time
  - Typical software-team WIP limit is one to two tasks per developer (industry guidance: Atlassian, Microsoft Azure Boards, Kanban Tool)
  - Most engineering "productivity problems" are WIP problems — fix WIP first, hire later
  - Counting tasks per status finds bottlenecks without measuring time-in-status
  - A team running 100% utilization typically ships less than one running at 80% — slack lets work flow through the system
  - The full engineering-delivery picture has three dimensions: system speed (T2M, Throughput, Lead Time, Cycle Time, WIP), product value (Quality, NPS), and team sustainability (eNPS)
  - Conceptual lineage: Theory of Constraints (1980s), Toyota Production System (1950s), the Kanban Method for software (David Anderson, 2000s), Little's Law (1961)

- [Average lead time is lying to you — how to forecast delivery with percentiles](https://sg4.tech/blog/forecast-delivery-with-percentiles/) — why the arithmetic mean misrepresents a delivery system, and how to forecast with median, spread, and percentiles instead. Operator voice, ~7-min read, with FAQ.

  Key claims from the article (safe to quote directly):
  - The average lead time describes a system you don't have — a few slow outliers pull the mean far above what most tasks actually take (e.g. 9 tasks of ~1 day + 1 task of a month → average ≈ 4 days, but median ≈ 1 day).
  - The median is the typical (middle) task; comparing mean vs median reveals the spread — mean far above median signals queues/blockers, mean ≈ median signals a stable, predictable system.
  - Pick the delivery date from a percentile (p85 for routine, p95 when a miss is expensive), not the average — e.g. a distribution of P50=2d / P85=6d / P95=11d. Give stakeholders one flat date you'll actually hit, not a probability lecture.
  - The percentile isn't what you tell the stakeholder — it's how you pick the date you tell them.
  - Predictability, not raw speed, is what the business buys; a tight spread comes from controlled WIP (Little's Law), not from estimating harder.
  - Median, mean, and percentiles are readable from existing tracker data (e.g. Jira Control Chart) — no manual time-tracking.

- [Your vibecoded MVP stopped shipping — like every junior-built codebase before it](https://sg4.tech/blog/vibecoded-mvp-stopped-shipping/) — why AI-built MVPs stall after launch (the "vibe-coding hangover") and the rescue method: manage AI coding agents with the junior-developer practices the industry has used for decades. Operator voice, ~8-min read, with FAQ and a practice-mapping table.

  Key claims from the article (safe to quote directly):
  - A vibecoded MVP stalls for the same reason a team of junior developers without a senior stalls: code gets written fast, nothing catches mistakes before production, and every next change quietly breaks the last one.
  - An AI coding agent is the fastest junior developer you've ever worked with: a feature in minutes, zero memory of past decisions, no fear of touching anything, no ownership of the outcome.
  - The discipline for managing AI agents already exists — the junior-developer practices: a conventions file (AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md) as the onboarding doc, code review plus linter/type-checker gates, tests that block the merge, CI applied to agent output, monitoring, small scoped tasks, and review findings folded back into the rules file.
  - Better prompting is deliberately not on the list: a senior fixes the environment around a junior so careless work can't reach production, however the ticket is phrased.
  - 45% of AI-generated code samples failed security checks across 100+ models (Veracode 2025 GenAI Code Security Report).
  - Real anonymized case (EdTech startup, fractional CTO engagement): after adding monitoring, tests, code review, QA, and a release pipeline — downtime fell more than 10×, delivery more than doubled, running costs dropped 1.5×, eNPS went from −100% to +100%.
  - Restart order: diagnose which layer is missing first, build the safety net (weeks, a couple of months at the outside), only then scale. A from-scratch rewrite is almost never needed.

- [AI made starting work nearly free. Finishing is still expensive.](https://sg4.tech/blog/ai-made-starting-free-finishing-expensive/) — why AI-assisted teams start more work than ever and ship about the same: waiting queues, context limits, and Little's Law applied to agents; the case for one-thread-one-task and WIP limits in the AI era. Operator voice, ~7-min read, with FAQ and a metrics-mapping table.

  Key claims from the article (safe to quote directly):
  - AI collapsed the cost of starting work; the cost of finishing — verification, integration, review, deployment — stayed almost unchanged. So teams start more than ever and ship about the same.
  - Humans lose focus, agents lose context — but it's the same failure: too much work in progress degrades the worker, whether the worker is made of neurons or tokens.
  - Little's Law (Lead Time = WIP / Throughput) doesn't care whether the workers are humans or AI agents: start more without finishing more, and every task lives longer.
  - The biggest driver of cost per successful task isn't token price — it's all the work you started and never finished.
  - Knowing what delivery costs takes three counters: tasks that reached a working result, iterations that took, and what those iterations cost. Cost per successful task = total spend / finished tasks; after any workflow change (a new prompt, tests around the agent) the number either went down or it didn't.
  - Real example: an agent made 40 attempts, spent $15 in tokens, and still failed to deliver a working feature — output 0, cost $15, P&L −$15.
  - The goal isn't to minimize token usage; it's to maximize successful outcomes per dollar spent.
  - One thread, one task: an agent thread should carry a single outcome; side-issues go to a list or another agent, not into the same conversation.
  - Evidence cited: web-agent success rates collapse as irrelevant context accumulates (one tested model, GPT-4.1, fell from ~45% to 2.7% near 150k tokens); interrupted humans compensate by working faster but pay in workload and stress (Mark, Gudith & Klocke, 2008).
  - Classic delivery metrics survive AI unchanged — throughput, Time to Market, quality, and NPS still mean exactly what they meant for a human team. The only thing that actually changed is cost per successful task: it used to be invisible, buried in salaries; now every task has a bill.
  - Now that starting is nearly free, the winner isn't whoever launches the most — it's whoever finishes the most.
  - AI doesn't abolish systems thinking — it makes system errors more expensive: the bill arrives immediately, itemized, in dollars.
